---
title: "08 ¬∑ The Unit Circle"
subtitle: "Trig for all angles ‚ÄĒ and the entry of radians"
lesson-id: "t3.unit-circle"
tier: 3
prerequisites: ["t3.trig-ratios", "t2.coordinate-plane"]
unlocks: ["t3.trig-identities"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [07 ¬∑ Trig Ratios](../07-trig-ratios/index.qmd) and
   [T2 L04 ‚ÄĒ Coordinate Plane](../../tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $\sin 30¬į = 1/2$, $\cos 60¬į = 1/2$, $\sin 90¬į = 1$?
3. Recall the equation of a unit circle from [03 ‚ÄĒ Conics](../03-conic-sections/index.qmd):
   $x^2 + y^2 = 1$.
:::

## Touch

Drag the angle around the unit circle. Watch the point's coordinates
$(x, y) = (\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$ update.

```{ojs}
viewof angDeg = Inputs.range([0, 720], {value: 30, step: 5, label: "Angle őł (degrees):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 480, H = 480, scale = 180, ox = W/2, oy = H/2;
  const t = angDeg * Math.PI / 180;
  const px = ox + scale * Math.cos(t);
  const py_ = oy - scale * Math.sin(t);

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:520px; display:block; margin: 0 auto; background:#fafafa;");

  // axes
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", 20).attr("x2", W - 20).attr("y1", oy).attr("y2", oy).attr("stroke", "#bbb");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", ox).attr("x2", ox).attr("y1", 20).attr("y2", H - 20).attr("stroke", "#bbb");
  // unit circle
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", ox).attr("cy", oy).attr("r", scale).attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#666").attr("stroke-width", 1);
  // arc
  const reducedT = ((t % (2 * Math.PI)) + 2 * Math.PI) % (2 * Math.PI);
  const arcR = 30, large = reducedT > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
  svg.append("path").attr("d", `M ${ox + arcR},${oy} A ${arcR},${arcR} 0 ${large} 0 ${ox + arcR * Math.cos(-reducedT)},${oy + arcR * Math.sin(-reducedT)}`)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // dashed lines for cos and sin
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px).attr("y1", oy).attr("x2", px).attr("y2", py_).attr("stroke", "#1565c0").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3 3");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", py_).attr("x2", px).attr("y2", py_).attr("stroke", "#2e7d32").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3 3");
  // radius
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy).attr("x2", px).attr("y2", py_).attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // point
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px).attr("cy", py_).attr("r", 7).attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", px + 8).attr("y", py_ - 6).attr("font-size", "12").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#c2185b").text(`(${Math.cos(t).toFixed(2)}, ${Math.sin(t).toFixed(2)})`);

  // labels at axis intersections
  const ringLabels = [["1", scale + 16, oy - 4], ["‚ąí1", -scale - 18, oy - 4], ["1", -8, -scale - 6], ["‚ąí1", -10, scale + 16]];
  for (const [lbl, dx, dy] of ringLabels)
    svg.append("text").attr("x", ox + dx).attr("y", oy + dy).attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", "#666").text(lbl);

  // info
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 20).attr("y", 30).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#1565c0").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`cos őł = ${Math.cos(t).toFixed(3)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 20).attr("y", 50).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#2e7d32").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`sin őł = ${Math.sin(t).toFixed(3)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W - 80).attr("y", 30).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`őł = ${angDeg}¬į`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W - 80).attr("y", 50).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").text(`= ${(t / Math.PI).toFixed(3)} ŌÄ rad`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

The **unit circle** has radius 1 centred at the origin. For angle
$\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the positive $x$-axis, the
point on the circle is $(\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$.

This *defines* sin and cos for **every angle** ‚ÄĒ including angles
beyond 90¬į and negative angles.

## Wonder

1. Set $\theta = 0$. Where is the point? What are sin, cos?
2. Set $\theta = 180¬į$. Now $\theta = 270¬į$. What signs do sin and cos
   have?
3. Set $\theta = 360¬į$. Same as $0¬į$ ‚ÄĒ periodic. What about $720¬į$?

## Discover

### Problem 1

What are $\sin 90¬į, \cos 90¬į, \sin 180¬į, \cos 180¬į, \sin 270¬į, \cos
270¬į$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚ÄĒ a nudge</summary>
Read off the unit-circle coordinates at each cardinal angle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- 90¬į: point is $(0, 1)$. $\cos = 0, \sin = 1$.
- 180¬į: point is $(-1, 0)$. $\cos = -1, \sin = 0$.
- 270¬į: point is $(0, -1)$. $\cos = 0, \sin = -1$.

The unit-circle definition extends sin and cos to all angles ‚ÄĒ and
they take negative values when the point is in the left or bottom
half.
</details>

### Problem 2

What's $\sin 150¬į$? (Hint: $150¬į = 180¬į - 30¬į$. The sine has the same
*magnitude* as $\sin 30¬į$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 150¬į = \sin 30¬į = 1/2$.

The point at $150¬į$ is the *reflection* of the point at $30¬į$ across
the $y$-axis. Same height (sine), opposite horizontal position
(cosine). So $\cos 150¬į = -\cos 30¬į = -\sqrt{3}/2$.

This is the "reference angle" technique: any angle's trig values
match those of its reference angle (the acute angle to the nearest
$x$-axis), with signs determined by the *quadrant*.
</details>

### Problem 3

Set $\theta = 360¬į$. Same point as $0¬į$. What about $\theta = 720¬į$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both same as $0¬į$. Sin and cos are **periodic with period $360¬į$**:
$$\sin(\theta + 360¬į) = \sin \theta, \quad \cos(\theta + 360¬į) = \cos \theta.$$

This periodicity is *the* defining feature of trig functions ‚ÄĒ and
why they describe waves, oscillations, and rotations.
</details>

### Problem 4

What's $\sin(-30¬į)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚ÄĒ a nudge</summary>
Negative angle = clockwise rotation. The point lands at the
reflection of the $30¬į$ point across the $x$-axis.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(-30¬į) = -1/2$.

**Sin is odd:** $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin \theta$.
**Cos is even:** $\cos(-\theta) = \cos \theta$.

These reflect the unit-circle reflection symmetry: across the $x$-axis,
$y$ flips sign (sin) but $x$ doesn't (cos).
</details>

### Problem 5

**Radians.** A radian is the angle that subtends an arc length equal
to the radius. A full revolution = $2\pi$ rad (since the
circumference of the unit circle is $2\pi$). So:

- $360¬į = 2\pi$ rad.
- $180¬į = \pi$ rad.
- $90¬į = \pi/2$ rad.
- $60¬į = \pi/3$ rad.
- $30¬į = \pi/6$ rad.

What's $45¬į$ in radians? $\sin(\pi/4) = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$45¬į = \pi/4$ rad. $\sin(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}/2$ (same as $\sin 45¬į$).

**Conversion:** $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot
\dfrac{\pi}{180}$ and vice versa.

Radians become essential in calculus (Tier 4), where $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}
\sin \theta = \cos \theta$ *only* if $\theta$ is in radians. From now
on, mathematicians prefer radians.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The first known trigonometric table was compiled around 140 BCE by
**Hipparchus of Nicaea**, who tabulated the *chord* of an angle in a
circle of fixed radius ‚ÄĒ the straight-line distance across the arc.
The Greeks worked with chords; the Indians worked with half-chords
(jyńĀ); modern trigonometry, born in Renaissance Europe, settled on
the unit circle and the half-chord (sine) we use today. The word
*radian* itself only appeared in 1873, coined at Queen's College
Belfast by **James Thomson** (older brother of Lord Kelvin) ‚ÄĒ a
late arrival for a unit you cannot do calculus without.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The unit circle promotes sin and cos from *triangle ratios* to
*coordinate functions*: $\cos\theta$ is the $x$-coordinate of the
rotating point, $\sin\theta$ is the $y$-coordinate. Suddenly the
ratios make sense for any angle ‚ÄĒ past 90¬į, past 360¬į, negative ‚ÄĒ
and they become periodic by construction. The Pythagorean
identity is the unit circle's equation in disguise.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **unit circle** is the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ centred at the
origin. For an angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the
positive $x$-axis, the point on the unit circle is
$$(\cos \theta, \sin \theta).$$

This **defines sin and cos for all real $\theta$** (positive,
negative, more than $360¬į$).

**Periodicity:** $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin \theta$, $\cos(\theta +
2\pi) = \cos \theta$.

**Symmetry:** $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin \theta$ (odd), $\cos(-\theta) =
\cos \theta$ (even).

**Quadrant signs:**

| Quadrant | sin | cos | tan |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| I (0¬į‚Äď90¬į) | + | + | + |
| II (90¬į‚Äď180¬į) | + | ‚ąí | ‚ąí |
| III (180¬į‚Äď270¬į) | ‚ąí | ‚ąí | + |
| IV (270¬į‚Äď360¬į) | ‚ąí | + | ‚ąí |

**Radians:** $360¬į = 2\pi$ rad, $180¬į = \pi$ rad. The conversion is
$\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi/180$.

:::

> *On the unit circle, sin and cos are y and x of the rotating point;*
> *Periodic, sign-flipping by quadrant, defined for every angle;*
> *Radians scale by ŌÄ/180 from degrees ‚ÄĒ the natural angular unit.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\sin 210¬į$ and $\cos 210¬į$.

A competent solver:

1. **Quadrant:** $210¬į$ is in QIII (between 180¬į and 270¬į). Sin
   negative, cos negative.
2. **Reference angle:** $210¬į - 180¬į = 30¬į$.
3. **Apply signs:** $\sin 210¬į = -\sin 30¬į = -1/2$. $\cos 210¬į = -\cos
   30¬į = -\sqrt{3}/2$.

**Answer: $\sin 210¬į = -1/2$, $\cos 210¬į = -\sqrt{3}/2$.**

### Practice ‚ÄĒ Warm

**W1.** $\cos 0¬į, \sin 90¬į, \cos 180¬į, \sin 270¬į$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1, 1, -1, -1$.
</details>

**W2.** Convert: $90¬į$ to rad. $\pi/3$ to degrees.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi/2$ and $60¬į$.
</details>

**W3.** $\sin(-60¬į)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-\sqrt{3}/2$ (sin is odd).
</details>

### Practice ‚ÄĒ Working

**P1.** Compute $\sin 135¬į$ and $\cos 135¬į$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚ÄĒ a nudge</summary>
QII. Reference angle 45¬į.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 135¬į = \sin 45¬į = \sqrt{2}/2$.
$\cos 135¬į = -\cos 45¬į = -\sqrt{2}/2$.
</details>

**P2.** Compute $\sin 300¬į$ and $\cos 300¬į$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
QIV. Reference: 60¬į. Sin negative, cos positive.
$\sin 300¬į = -\sqrt{3}/2$, $\cos 300¬į = 1/2$.
</details>

**P3.** Verify the **Pythagorean identity** $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2
\theta = 1$ for $\theta = 240¬į$ using the unit circle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 240¬į = -\sqrt{3}/2, \cos 240¬į = -1/2$.
$\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1$ ‚úď.

The identity is just "the point lies on the unit circle" ‚ÄĒ i.e.,
$y^2 + x^2 = 1^2$. Pythagoras lurking.
</details>

### Practice ‚ÄĒ Stretch

**S1.** A point on the unit circle is **rotated 60¬į** counter-
clockwise. If it started at angle 100¬į, where is it now? What are its
new coordinates?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
New angle: $100¬į + 60¬į = 160¬į$. Coordinates: $(\cos 160¬į, \sin 160¬į)
\approx (-0.940, 0.342)$.

**Rotation = adding to the angle.** This is why complex multiplication
($e^{i \alpha} \cdot e^{i \beta} = e^{i(\alpha + \beta)}$, Tier 6) is
the natural way to compose rotations ‚ÄĒ and why every game engine
under the hood uses *quaternions* (Tier 7), the 4D extension.
</details>

**S2.** A swinging pendulum has angle from vertical $\theta(t) =
A \cos(\omega t)$, where $A$ is the *amplitude* and $\omega$ is the
*angular frequency*. Why does cosine model it perfectly?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Because the pendulum's motion is uniform circular motion projected
onto a line.** For small angles, the pendulum's deflection traces a
horizontal sinusoid in time. The unit-circle picture *is* the
underlying physics.

Ditto for AC electricity (a sinusoid in time), audio (sums of
sinusoids), light waves, ocean tides ‚ÄĒ all are projections of
*rotation* onto an axis. This is one of the most consequential
unifications in physics: rotation and oscillation are the same thing
seen differently.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Sin and cos are between 0 and 1."** **False** ‚ÄĒ they are
   between $-1$ and $+1$. Negative values appear in QII, QIII, QIV.
2. **"Degrees and radians are interchangeable."** They measure the
   same thing but use different units. Calculator mode matters!
3. **"Radians need $\pi$ in them."** Not always ‚ÄĒ radians can be any
   real number. $1$ radian $\approx 57.3¬į$.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **On the unit [circle](/glossary.qmd#circle), the point at
   [angle](/glossary.qmd#angle) $\theta$ has coordinates
   $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ ‚ÄĒ that *defines* sine and cosine for
   every angle, not just the acute ones.**
2. **Sine and cosine are periodic with period $2\pi$ (or $360¬į$);
   their signs in each quadrant follow from where the point sits.**
3. **Radians measure angles by arc length on the unit circle; full
   turn $= 2\pi$, and they are the unit calculus speaks natively.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you locate $(\cos 120¬į, \sin 120¬į)$ on the unit circle by
   eye?
2. Without scrolling back, what is $\cos 270¬į$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: 0.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[09 ¬∑ Trig Identities](../09-trig-identities/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (pendulum) ‚ÄĒ a deep connection.
- **0‚Äď1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[07 ¬∑ Trig Ratios](../07-trig-ratios/index.qmd)**
- **[T2 L04 ‚ÄĒ Coordinate Plane](../../tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[09 ¬∑ Trig Identities](../09-trig-identities/index.qmd)** ‚ÄĒ many
  identities follow from unit-circle symmetries.
- **Tier 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculus** ‚ÄĒ derivatives of sin/cos, written most
  cleanly in radians.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** All 2D rotations are unit-circle traversals.
Direction/orientation/aim/recoil are angles. Quaternions (Tier 7) are
the 3D extension.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics & engineering.** Simple harmonic motion, AC current,
pendulums, waves ‚ÄĒ every sinusoid in physics is a unit-circle
projection.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Euler's formula $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta
+ i\sin\theta$ (Tier 6) makes the unit circle the natural home of
complex numbers. The deepest unification in elementary mathematics.
:::
